critic_raw:
This Deep RL traffic signal control design has a fundamental training paradigm problem and a reward design flaw that are both structural.

The RL agent is trained in 'a deterministic traffic simulator initialized with statistical properties from the training data.' The simulator is not the real intersection — it is a model of it. This introduces a sim-to-real gap: if the simulator's queueing model, turn ratios, or pedestrian cycle assumptions do not perfectly match real-world dynamics, the learned policy will be optimized for simulator behavior, not real-world behavior. The design evaluates the agent on 'real-world traffic data from the test period,' but if the agent is only trained in simulation, the test evaluation is the first contact with real traffic dynamics. Any systematic modeling error in the simulator (e.g., incorrect saturation flow rates, simplified pedestrian crossing models, missing emergency vehicle preemption) will appear as test performance degradation that cannot be attributed to the hypothesis under test.

Wait times are 'computed from detector occupancy data using standard queueing approximations.' This is a derived metric, not a direct measurement. Queueing approximations (e.g., Webster's formula, HCM delay equations) assume specific arrival distributions (often Poisson) and steady-state conditions. At real urban intersections during peak hours, oversaturated conditions violate these assumptions. The reported wait times in the primary metric may systematically underestimate or overestimate true wait times for oversaturated approaches, and this error will be present in both baseline and treatment conditions — but if the RL agent learns to optimize the proxy metric (simulator reward based on approximate queueing) while the baseline uses fixed timings, the RL agent may have an optimization advantage on the proxy metric that does not translate to actual vehicle wait time reduction.

The reward signal is 'negative average wait time plus a small penalty for phase transitions.' The wait time component uses the same queueing approximation as the evaluation metric, which means the agent is optimized directly on the measurement proxy rather than the true outcome. If the approximation systematically underweights certain vehicle classes (e.g., heavy vehicles with longer saturation headways) or certain turning movements (e.g., unprotected lefts with large gaps), the policy will deprioritize these classes, and the evaluation metric (using the same approximation) will not detect this.

The 'two-tailed t-test at α=0.05' for the primary hypothesis test is inappropriate for autocorrelated time series data. Peak-hour wait times in successive days are highly correlated (Monday patterns repeat weekly, weather effects persist). The design proposes block bootstrap with weekly blocks for confidence intervals, but then uses a standard t-test for significance — these are inconsistent. The standard t-test assumes independent observations; weekly block bootstrap acknowledges they are not. The significance test should use a method consistent with the autocorrelation structure acknowledged in the bootstrap design.
all_issues_raised: The RL agent is trained entirely in a deterministic simulator and evaluated on real-world test data; any systematic modeling error in the simulator (saturation flow rates, turn ratios, pedestrian cycles) will cause the agent to optimize for simulator dynamics rather than real traffic, and the test evaluation represents the first contact with actual intersection physics.

Wait times are derived from queueing approximations that assume Poisson arrivals and steady-state conditions; at oversaturated urban intersections during peak hours these assumptions fail, so both the reward signal and the evaluation metric measure the same proxy rather than true vehicle wait time, potentially giving the RL agent an advantage on the proxy that does not reflect real-world improvement.

The design proposes block bootstrap for confidence intervals (acknowledging temporal autocorrelation) but uses a standard two-tailed t-test for the primary significance test, which assumes independent observations; these procedures are internally inconsistent, and the t-test will produce anti-conservative p-values due to the autocorrelation structure.

The seasonal confound control assumes the test period can be aligned to the 'same calendar months in the baseline year where possible,' but with training in months 1-12 and test in months 16-18, year-over-year alignment is only achievable with a longer baseline period; if calendar alignment is infeasible, the seasonal indicator features are a covariate adjustment rather than a control, and residual seasonal confounding can inflate or deflate the apparent RL advantage.
all_issues_adjudicated: The RL agent is trained entirely in a deterministic simulator and evaluated on real-world test data; any systematic modeling error in the simulator (saturation flow rates, turn ratios, pedestrian cycles) will cause the agent to optimize for simulator dynamics rather than real traffic, and the test evaluation represents the first contact with actual intersection physics.

Wait times are derived from queueing approximations that assume Poisson arrivals and steady-state conditions; at oversaturated urban intersections during peak hours these assumptions fail, so both the reward signal and the evaluation metric measure the same proxy rather than true vehicle wait time, potentially giving the RL agent an advantage on the proxy that does not reflect real-world improvement.

The design proposes block bootstrap for confidence intervals (acknowledging temporal autocorrelation) but uses a standard two-tailed t-test for the primary significance test, which assumes independent observations; these procedures are internally inconsistent, and the t-test will produce anti-conservative p-values due to the autocorrelation structure.

The seasonal confound control assumes the test period can be aligned to the 'same calendar months in the baseline year where possible,' but with training in months 1-12 and test in months 16-18, year-over-year alignment is only achievable with a longer baseline period; if calendar alignment is infeasible, the seasonal indicator features are a covariate adjustment rather than a control, and residual seasonal confounding can inflate or deflate the apparent RL advantage.
